summary: High Renaissance Italian painter from Urbino. Most commonly identified by The School of Athens, La Disputa, and The Transfiguration. Student of Perugino. Painted numerous Madonnas (Goldfinch, Pinks, Sistine). The Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood was named in opposition to his influence. Ingres painted him with La Fornarina. Died at 37.

works.name: The School of Athens (1509–1511)
works.description: Fresco in the Stanza della Segnatura, Vatican. Plato points upward while Aristotle gestures outward. Bramante modeled as Euclid using a compass at bottom right. Self-portrait of Raphael appears at right. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Plato points upward while talking to Aristotle among a group of philosophers

works.name: La Disputa (Disputation of the Holy Sacrament)
works.description: Fresco depicting Aquinas, Jerome, and theologians debating transubstantiation. Four putti hold the gospels below Christ's feet. Altar holds an ostensory.

clues.clue: Depicts Aquinas, Jerome, and theologians debating transubstantiation; four putti hold the gospels

works.name: The Transfiguration (1516–1520)
works.description: Raphael's final painting. Upper half shows Moses and Elijah appearing; lower half shows apostles attempting to cure a possessed boy. A pope kept it instead of sending to Narbonne Cathedral.

clues.clue: Final painting; shows Moses and Elijah appearing above; apostles attempting to cure a possessed boy below

this artist unusually included the apostles attempting to cure a possessed boy in the lower foreground

works.name: Madonnas (Goldfinch, Pinks, Sistine Madonna, Marriage of the Virgin)
works.description: Raphael painted numerous Madonna scenes. Madonna of the Goldfinch shows infants Jesus and John the Baptist playing with the title bird. Marriage of the Virgin adapts Perugino's version with a Dome of the Rock-inspired building.

clues.clue: Madonna of the Goldfinch: Mary holds a book while infants Jesus and John the Baptist play with the title bird
examples: Mary holds a book in a pastoral landscape as she looks down at infants Jesus and John the Baptist, who are playing with the title bird

clues.clue: Marriage of the Virgin: adapts mentor Perugino's version; building inspired by Dome of the Rock
examples: this artist added more detail to a building inspired by the Dome of the Rock in the background of a wedding

clues.clue: Madonna of the Pinks: central figures hold carnations; corner window looks onto a ruin

recursive_suggestions: Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood — frequently asked about, named after Raphael
The School of Athens — has its own answerline
comprehensive_summary:
Raphael (Raffaello Sanzio da Urbino) was a High Renaissance Italian painter, student of Perugino. His most famous work is The School of Athens, a fresco in the Vatican's Stanza della Segnatura showing Plato pointing upward and Aristotle gesturing outward among philosophers, with Bramante modeled as a figure using a compass at the bottom right. The same room holds La Disputa (depicting theologians debating transubstantiation with four putti holding the gospels) and The Parnassus (Apollo playing violin among poets and Muses).

His final painting, The Transfiguration (1520), shows Moses and Elijah appearing above while apostles attempt to cure a possessed boy below. A pope kept it instead of sending it to Narbonne Cathedral. He painted numerous Madonnas: Madonna of the Goldfinch (infants Jesus and John the Baptist play with the bird), Marriage of the Virgin (adapts Perugino's version with Dome of the Rock-inspired building), and Madonna of the Pinks. He designed tapestries depicting the lives of Peter and Paul known as the 'Raphael Cartoons.'

The Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood was named in opposition to his influence; Millais and Rossetti believed he 'ruined art.' Joshua Reynolds's praise of Raphael in his Discourses led to the nickname 'Sir Sloshua.' Ingres painted Raphael and his mistress La Fornarina five times. Marcantonio Raimondo's engraving of Raphael's Judgement of Paris inspired Manet's Luncheon on the Grass. He inspired the German Nazarene movement; Benjamin West was called the 'American Raphael.'
